title: Lesson 8: The Grace Cycle to Forgiveness
description: Four words explain what God did for you at the cross: Grace, Mercy, Justified, and Righteous. Through an interactive diagram you will build step by step, you will see how these four realities work together in a cycle that starts with God's love and flows through you to others.

blocks.text: In the last lesson, you traced the one storyline that holds the entire Bible together, from creation to new creation. You saw that every page points to Jesus. Now we are going to zoom in on what Jesus actually accomplished for you at the cross.

For by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God, not a result of works, so that no one may boast. For we are his workmanship, created in Christ Jesus for good works, which God prepared beforehand, that we should walk in them.

Read that passage one more time, slowly. Paul says three things that will reshape how you understand everything God has done for you. First, salvation is a gift. You did not earn it. Second, it came by grace through faith, not by anything you did. Third, God did not save you so you could sit still. He saved you so that His grace would flow through you and into the lives of the people around you.

This lesson is built around four words that every believer should know: Grace, Mercy, Justified, and Righteous. These are big church words, but they describe something so simple and so good that once you see it, you will never forget it.
Four Words That Change Everything

Grace means getting a gift you do not deserve. You did nothing to earn it, and you could never pay it back. Mercy means not getting the punishment you do deserve. You broke the law, you earned the sentence, but the judge does not punish you. Justified means your punishment has been paid. Someone else stepped in and settled your debt. Righteous means you are now seen as perfect before God, not because you became perfect overnight, but because Jesus' perfect record was placed on your account.
Put those four together and you have the whole story of what happened to you at the cross. But before we open the diagram, picture it like this.
The Fruit Tree

A young boy sneaks into his neighbor's yard and climbs a new fruit tree in the back corner. He swings from the branches, snaps half the limbs, and by the time he drops to the ground the tree is ruined. The neighbor walks outside, looks at the damage, and says, "Somebody is going to pay for this." The boy's older brother hears what happened. Without being asked, he empties his entire savings account and buys the neighbor a brand-new tree. The debt is paid. The boy did not pay it, but it is paid. And then the father, who watched the whole thing, picks the boy up, puts him in the car, and takes him to the amusement park. Not because the boy earned a reward, but because the father delights in his son and the debt is no longer standing between them. That is mercy, justification, righteousness, and grace, all in one story. The neighbor's wrath is satisfied. The brother pays what the boy could never pay. And the father pours out an undeserved gift on a kid who broke everything he touched.
The Cycle of Grace

The interactive tool below walks you through each of these four realities one step at a time, and then shows you what happens when the cycle comes back around. Tap Begin to start, then use Next to move through each stop. Watch how the arrows connect, and notice what appears in the center when you reach the final step.
Walking Through the Five Stops

Stop 1: Mercy. The neighbor walks outside and sees a ruined tree. He has every right to call the police, press charges, and make the boy pay. But he does not. He looks at the boy and chooses not to bring the hammer down. That is mercy. God looked at your sin and had every right to condemn you. Instead, He responded with compassion. Mercy is not getting the punishment you do deserve.

Stop 2: Justified. But mercy alone does not explain how the neighbor's loss gets covered. The tree is still broken. Someone still has to pay. That is when the older brother steps in. Without being asked, he empties his savings and buys the neighbor a brand-new tree. Every penny of the cost lands on him, not the boy. That is justification. Jesus, your Older Brother, stepped into the courtroom and took your sentence. He was crushed so you could be cleared. "Just-if-I'd" never sinned. The debt is not overlooked. It is paid in full by someone else.

Stop 3: Righteous. Now look at the neighbor's yard. He does not see a patched-up stump or a half-repaired mess. He sees a brand-new tree, whole and beautiful, as if nothing was ever broken. That is righteousness. God did not just erase your record. He replaced it. The perfect, spotless life that Jesus lived was credited to your account. Theologians call this The Great Exchange.
For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God.

Stop 4: Grace. Now the father does something that makes no earthly sense. He picks the boy up, puts him in the car, and takes him to the amusement park. The boy who broke everything gets a reward he never earned. That is grace. God does not just forgive you and send you on your way. He pours out gift after gift: peace with God, access to His presence, hope, the Holy Spirit, a future inheritance. None of it earned. All of it grace.

Stop 5: The Cycle Comes Back Around. Years later, the boy watches his own little brother break something in the neighbor's yard. And instead of yelling, instead of demanding payback, he remembers what was done for him. He shows his little brother the same mercy he received. He covers the cost. He takes him out for ice cream. That is how the cycle works. Look at the diagram: when the arrow reaches Grace, it does not stop. It curves back toward Mercy. Those who have received mercy become people who show mercy. Those who have been given grace become people who live grace. The gift was never meant to stop with you.
Be kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, as God in Christ forgave you.
8.1 - Which of the five stops in the diagram hit you the hardest? Why?

8.2 - Think about the fruit tree story. Have you been living more like the boy who thinks he still owes a debt, or like the boy whose brother already paid it? What would change if you really believed the debt was settled?
8.3 - Who in your life needs you to show them the mercy or grace you have received from God? What would that look like this week?
There Was Another Fruit Tree

Here is something you may not have noticed. The story of the Bible begins with a boy and a tree too. In Genesis 3, Adam reached for the fruit of a tree he was told not to touch, and he broke everything. Sin, shame, and death entered the world because of what happened at that tree. And God looked at the wreckage and said, "Somebody is going to pay for this." Thousands of years later, Jesus, the true Older Brother, stretched out His arms on another tree and paid for it all. The cross is called "the tree" in Scripture (1 Peter 2:24). One tree broke the world. Another tree healed it. And now the Father delights to take you, the one who broke everything, and pour out every good gift He has. That is the grace cycle. That is the gospel. And it all happened on a tree.
Closing Reflections
What is one key thing you learned from this lesson, and what next step can you take this week?
What have you learned about God and yourself recently in your Daily Devotions?
What is a prayer request for this week?
